failures.fix: Emit more specific nature sub-tags alongside "nature" (e.g. wildlife, ecology, ocean, animals) to strengthen the topic signal within the documentary space; also raise anchor_weight above 1.0 when both require_genres and genome_tags are simultaneously set (topic-constrained genre query), since the 5 strong nature-doc anchors at weight 1.0 still cannot pull results away from the dominant crime/politics documentary cluster.
failures.rationale: The Documentary genre constraint is applied correctly and the "nature" tag is in-vocab, but only 2 of 15 recs (The Blue Planet, Human Planet) are actual nature documentaries — the remainder are crime, political, war, and sports docs — because the single "nature" tag and five nature-doc anchors at weight 1.0 are insufficient to separate the nature subspace from the dominant non-nature documentary cluster in the model's embedding space.

failures.fix: Add extraction rule: when user says "no violence/gore", avoid genome_tags associated with violent subgenres (especially "noir", which strongly implies crime violence). Instead weight toward "dialogue driven", "cerebral", "psychological", "character study". Document that "noir" as a tag is anti-intent for no-violence requests and must be suppressed.
failures.rationale: Injecting "noir" as a genome tag directly contradicts the "no violence" soft constraint — it pulls the top results toward some of Film-Noir's most famously violent entries (Kiss Me Deadly at #1, The Big Heat at #2, Rififi at #4), causing the recommendation list to clearly violate the user's stated preference.

failures.fix: Limit genome_tags to 1-2 terms for generic soft-genre requests with no named titles; enforce closed-vocab so 'heartfelt' (and similar unlisted tags) cannot be emitted; consider capping anchor_weight below 1.0 or reducing anchor count when the request has no title seeds, to prevent a Bergman/Wong Kar-wai arthouse cluster from swamping a plain romance signal.
failures.rationale: The extraction correctly captures liked_genres=Romance but emits "heartfelt" (out-of-vocab) and three arthouse-biasing tags that, together with 15 heavily arthouse anchors (Bergman, Antonioni, Wong Kar-wai) at anchor_weight=1.0, dominate the retrieval signal and push roughly 6 of the top-15 results into films without Romance in their genre list at all (The Great Beauty, Wild Strawberries, A Pigeon Sat on a Branch, Bad Education, Youth, Fading Gigolo), producing an extremely niche arthouse set for what is a generic mainstream request.

failures.fix: Add a prompt rule: for bare soft-genre requests with no explicit mood language, emit at most 1-2 genome_tags and only when clearly implied; never add "surreal", "dreamlike", or "atmospheric" to a plain "I enjoy Fantasy and Adventure" request — those tags anchor into arthouse-horror clusters, not mainstream fantasy/adventure.
failures.rationale: The extraction correctly captured liked_genres but invented four unsupported mood tags (whimsical/dreamlike/atmospheric/surreal) that generated arthouse anchors (Eraserhead, Andalusian Dog, Meshes of the Afternoon, Nostalghia) at anchor_weight=1.0, producing a top-15 dominated by Horror/Drama/Crime art-house cinema with almost no Fantasy or Adventure titles.

failures.fix: Add a prompt rule: when the utterance indicates a family/multi-generational audience ("family movie night", "whole family", "kids and adults", "all ages"), include "Children" as a soft genre preference in the extraction alongside mood tags, so the retrieval anchors toward age-appropriate content rather than adult-oriented feel-good dramas.
failures.rationale: The extraction correctly captured the mood (feel-good, heartwarming, fun) but entirely missed mapping "whole family can watch" to a Children/family-appropriate genre signal, causing adult-themed films like Juno (#1), Love Actually (#7), Slumdog Millionaire (#8), Chocolat (#11), and Bridget Jones's Diary (#15) to appear prominently — roughly a third of the top-15 are unsuitable for a family movie night.

failures.fix: Boost genome-tag embedding weight relative to anchor-cluster pull when the tag is the primary semantic differentiator (no liked_items named); the "talking animals" signal is being drowned by the late-1990s Children-animation neighborhood pulled in by the Dr. Dolittle / Open Season / Zookeeper anchors, yielding generic children's movies instead of titles where talking animals are a central feature
failures.rationale: The extraction is faithful and clean (correct genre constraint, specific in-vocab tag, nothing invented), but the "talking animals" genome tag fails to differentiate within the Children genre cluster — results include Home Alone 3, Free Willy 3, Amazing Panda Adventure, and Air Bud, none of which feature talking animals, while clear genre-defining titles like Babe, Homeward Bound, or Charlotte's Web are absent.

failures.fix: When require_genres is set, select anchor films that satisfy BOTH the genome_tags AND the required genre (e.g. draw "space" anchors from Firefly/Westworld rather than SpaceCamp/Mission to Mars); anchors from pure-space films get filtered out by the Western constraint and contribute no useful ranking signal to the surviving Western corpus.
failures.rationale: The extraction is correct (require_genres=Western + genome_tags=["space"] faithfully encodes the intent), but the five "space" anchors are all pure Sci-Fi films with no Western tag, so after the Western genre filter is applied those anchors are irrelevant to every surviving candidate, leaving positions 3-15 filled by conventional Westerns with no space connection.

failures.fix: Add prompt rule: when a specific holiday (Christmas, Halloween, etc.) is the core request, emit ONLY tags tightly tied to that holiday (e.g. "christmas", "holiday") and suppress generic mood tags (feel-good, heartwarming, sentimental) that generate large pools of non-holiday anchors and drown out the holiday signal.
failures.rationale: The "christmas" tag was correctly identified but three co-emitted generic mood tags (feel-good, heartwarming, sentimental) generated ~15 non-Christmas feel-good anchors against only ~5 Christmas-specific anchors, completely overwhelming the holiday signal and yielding 15 feel-good dramas/documentaries (Lion, RBG, Hidden Figures, 13th, etc.) with zero Christmas movies in the top 15.
